title: Cyclosorus rufostramineus
intro: Cyclosorus rufostramineus, also known as the Copper-leaved Lipfern, is a type of fern native to tropical regions of Asia, characterized by its copper-colored fronds. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine, offering potential health benefits including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of arthritis, improve liver function, and protect against oxidative stress. Bioactive constituents found in Cyclosorus rufostramineus include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, the plant is often prepared as a decoction or tea, and its extracts have also been used in the formulation of capsules and supplements.
taxonomy: Cyclosorus rufostramineus belongs to the family Thelypteridaceae, commonly known as the marsh fern family. It is a species of fern in the genus Cyclosorus. This genus is part of the larger group of leptosporangiate ferns, also known as the 'true ferns'. The plant's classification is further supported by its position within the family Thelypteridaceae, which is a distinct group of ferns. The classification of Cyclosorus rufostramineus is also reflected in its botanical name, which follows the binomial nomenclature system used to classify plant species.
uses: Cyclosorus rufostramineus, also known as the Copper Shield Fern, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Traditionally, the plant is used to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory problems. In some Asian cultures, the plant is used to treat snake bites and other venomous injuries. However, more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness in treating these conditions. The plant's medicinal properties are thought to be due to the presence of compounds such as flavonoids and terpenoids.
